The main entry point for a UIKit or AppKit app is the app object, which handles the system-required initialization tasks.
Your app delegate is one of many controller objects you use to manage your app’s behavior.
UIKit and AppKit use a model-view-controller architecture to manage information.
This architecture separates the objects that manage your app’s data from the interface objects you use to present that data.
One special data object you use to manage content is the document object.
If your app manages document-based content, use the (UIKit) or (AppKit) type to manage your document’s data.

To avoid disrupting your Windows-based workflow, Apple provides tools to configure a remote Mac to build and debug the macOS version of your game from your PC.
Setting up a Mac to build your Windows code remotely requires changes to both the Mac and your PC.
To build on both platforms, you must have a CMake-based project, which provides the instructions that both systems need to build your code.
Download and install the on your Mac if your build process requires building DXIL shaders.
In System Settings, choose General > Sharing, and ensure that both Remote Management and Remote Login are turned on to allow VNC and SSH access, respectively.
If your network uses a firewall, make sure it allows Remote Management and Remote Login services, as well as TCP port forwarding over SSH.
Microsoft Visual Studio requires this configuration to copy files over the network.
With Visual Studio installed and set up, choose Tools > Options > CMake and select “Use CMake presets if available, otherwise use a CMakeSettings.json”.
Avoid opening any Visual Studio Solution files.
If your project doesn’t currently use CMake, adopt CMake as your native build system or implement a mechanism to drive your current build system through CMake for remote builds.
